verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Now as soon as it was day, there was no small stir among the soldiers, what was become of Peter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when Herod had sought for him, and found him not, he examined the keepers, and commanded that they should be put to death.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he went down from Judea to Cæsarea, and there abode.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Some persons, it is likely, are at a loss how to explain it, that God should quietly look on while (His) champions are put to death, and now again the soldiers on account of Peter: and yet it was possible for Him after (delivering) Peter to rescue them also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But it was not yet the time of judgment, so as to render to each according to his deserts.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And besides, it was not Peter that put them into his hands.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the thing that most annoyed him was the being mocked; just as in the case of his grandfather when he was deceived by the wise men, that was what made him (feel) cut to the heart—the being (eluded and) made ridiculous.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And having put them to the question,” it says, “he ordered them to be led away to execution.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet he had heard from them—for he had put them to the question—both that the chains had been left, and that he had taken his sandals, and that until that night he was with them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Having put them to the question:” but what did they conceal?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why then did they not themselves also flee?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He ordered them to be led away to execution:” and yet he ought to have marvelled, ought to have been astonished at this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The consequence is, by the death of these men (the thing), is made manifest to all: both his wickedness is exposed to view, and (it is made clear that) the wonder (is) of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he went down from Judea to Cæsarea, and there abode: and Herod was highly displeased with them of Tyre and Sidon: but they came with one accord to him, and, having made Blastus the king’s chamberlain their friend, desired peace; because their country was nourished by the king’s country.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And upon a set day Herod, arrayed in royal apparel, sat upon his throne, and made an oration unto them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the people gave a shout, saying, ‘It is the voice of a god, and not of a man,’ And immediately the angel of the Lord smote him, because he gave not God the glory: and he was eaten of worms, and gave up the ghost.” (v. xx.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 23.) * * But see how (the writer) here does not hide these things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why does he mention this history?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say, what has it to do with the Gospel, that Herod is incensed with the Tyrians and Sidonians?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not a small matter, even this, how immediately justice seized him; although not because of Peter, but because of his arrogant speaking.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet, it may be said, if those shouted, what is that to him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Because he accepted the acclamation, because he accounted himself to be worthy of the adoration.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Through him those most receive a lesson, who so thoughtlessly flattered him (al. οἱ κολακεύοντες).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe again, while both parties deserve punishment, this man is punished.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this is not the time of judgment, but He punishes him that had most to answer for, leaving the others to profit by this man’s fate.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the word of God,” it says, “grew,” i.e. in consequence of this, “and multiplied.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark God’s providential management?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But Barnabas and Saul returned from Jerusalem, when they had fulfilled their ministry, and took with them John, whose surname was Mark.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Now there were in the Church that was at Antioch, certain prophets and teachers; as Barnabas, and Simeon that was called Niger, and Lucius of Cyrene, and Manaën, which had been brought up with Herod the tetrarch, and Saul.” (ch. xiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He still mentions Barnabas first: for Paul was not yet famous, he had not yet wrought any sign.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “As they ministered to the Lord, and fasted, the Holy Ghost said, Separate Me Barnabas and Saul for the work whereunto I have called them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when they had fasted and prayed, and laid their hands on them, they sent them away.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What means, “Ministering?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Separate for Me,” it says, “Barnabas and Saul.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What means, “Separate for Me?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the work, for the Apostleship.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See again by what persons he is ordained (γυμνοτέρα.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Cat. σεμνοτέρα, “more awful.”)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By Lucius the Cyrenean and Manaën, or rather, by the Spirit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The less the persons, the more palpable the grace.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He is ordained henceforth to Apostleship, so as to preach with authority.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How then does he himself say, “Not from men, nor by man?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Because it was not man that called or brought him over: this is why he says, “Not from men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Neither by man,” that is, that he was not sent by this (man), but by the Spirit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore also (the writer) thus proceeds: “So they, being sent forth by the Holy Ghost, departed unto Seleucia; and from thence they sailed to Cyprus.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us look over again what has been said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when it was day,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if the Angel had brought out the soldiers also, along with Peter, it would have been thought a case of flight.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then why, you may ask, was it not otherwise managed?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, where is the harm?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now, if we see that they who have suffered unjustly, take no harm, we shall not raise these questions.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For why do you not say the same of James?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why did not (God) rescue him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “There was no small stir among the soldiers.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So (clearly) had they perceived nothing (of what had happened).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Lo, I take up the plea in their defence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The chains were there, and the keepers within, and the prison shut, nowhere a wall broken through, all told the same tale: the man had been carried off: why dost thou condemn them?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Had they wished to let him off, they would have done it before, or would have gone out with him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But he gave them money?” (ch. iii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And how should he, who had not to give even to a poor man, have the means to give to these?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And then neither had the chains been broken, nor were they loosed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He ought to have seen, that the thing was of God, and no work of man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he went down from Judea to Cæsarea, and there abode.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Herod was highly displeased with them of Tyre and Sidon,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He is now going to mention (a matter of) history: this is the reason why he adds the names, that it may be shown how he keeps to the truth in all things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And,” it says, “having made Blastus the king’s chamberlain their friend, they desired peace; because their country was nourished by the king’s country.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For probably there was a famine.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And on a set day,” etc. (Joseph.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Josephus also says this, that he fell into a lingering disease.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now the generality were not aware of this, but the Apostle sets it down: yet at the same time their ignorance was an advantage, in regard that they imputed what befell (Agrippa) to his putting James and the soldiers to death.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe, when he slew the Apostle, he did nothing of this sort but when (he slew) these; in fact he knew not what to say about it: as being at a loss, then, and feeling ashamed, “he went down from Judea to Cæsarea.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I suppose it was also to bring those (men of Tyre and Sidon) to apologize, that he withdrew (from Jerusalem): for with those he was incensed, while paying such court to these.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how vainglorious the man is: meaning to confer the boon upon them, he makes an harangue.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But Josephus says, that he was also arrayed in a splendid robe made of silver.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe both what flatterers those were, and what a high spirit was shown by the Apostles: the man whom the whole nation so courted, the same they held in contempt. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But observe again a great refreshing granted to them, and the numberless benefits accruing from the vengeance inflicted upon him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if this man, because it was said to him, “It is the voice of God and not of a man (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 22) although he said nothing himself, suffered such things: much more should Christ, had He not Himself been God (have suffered) for saying always as He did, “These words of mine are not Mine” (John xiv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 36) and, “Angels minister to Me,” and such like.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But that man ended His life by a shameful and miserable death, and thenceforth no more is seen of him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe him also, easily talked over even by Blastus, like a poor creature, soon incensed and again pacified, and on all occasions a slave of the populace, with nothing free and independent about him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But mark also the authority of the Holy Ghost: “As they ministered to the Lord, and fasted, the Holy Ghost said, Separate Me Barnabas and Saul.” (ch. xiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What being would have dared, if not of the same authority, to say this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Separate,” etc. But this is done, that they may not keep together among themselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Spirit saw that they had greater power, and were able to be sufficient for many.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And how did He speak to them?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Probably by prophets: therefore the writer premises, that there were prophets also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they were fasting and ministering: that thou mayest learn that there was need of great sobriety.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In Antioch he is ordained, where he preaches.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why did He not say, Separate for the Lord, but, “For me?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It shows that He is of one authority and power.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when they had fasted,” etc. Seest thou what a great thing fasting is?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “So they being sent forth by the Holy Ghost:” it shows that the Spirit did all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: A great, yes a great good is fasting: it is circumscribed by no limits.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When need was to ordain, then they fast: and to them while fasting, the Spirit spake.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus much only do I enjoin: (I say) not fast, but abstain from luxury.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us seek meats to nourish, not things to ruin us; seek meats for food, not occasions of diseases, of diseases both of soul and body: seek food which hath comfort, not luxury which is full of discomfort: the one is luxury, the other mischief; the one is pleasure, the other pain; the one is agreeable to nature, the other contrary to nature.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For say, if one should give thee hemlock juice to drink, would it not be against nature? if one should give thee logs and stones, wouldest thou not reject them?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of course, for they are against nature.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well, and so is luxury.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For just as in a city, under an invasion of enemies when there has been siege and tumult, great is the uproar, so is it in the soul, under invasion of wine and luxury.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Who hath woe? who hath tumults? who hath discomforts and babblings?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Are they not they that tarry long at the wine?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whose are bloodshot eyes?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Prov. xxiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But yet, say what we will, we shall not bring off those who give themselves up to luxury, unless we bring into conflict therewith a different affection.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And first, let us address ourselves to the women.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing uglier than a woman given to luxury, nothing uglier than a woman given to drink.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The bloom of her complexion is faded: the calm and mild expression of the eyes is rendered turbid, as when a cloud intercepts the rays of the sunshine.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is a vulgar, (ἀνελεύθερον) slave-like, thoroughly low-lived habit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How disgusting is a woman when from her breath you catch sour whiffs of fetid wine: a woman belching, giving out a fume (χυμὸν) of decomposing meats; herself weighed down, unable to keep upright; her face flushed with an unnatural red; yawning incessantly, and everything swimming in a mist before her eyes!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But not such, she that abstains from luxurious living: no (this abstinence makes her look) a more beautiful, well-bred (σωφρονεστέρα) woman.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For even to the body, the composure of the soul imparts a beauty of its own.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do not imagine that the impression of beauty results only from the bodily features.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Give me a handsome girl, but turbulent (τεταραγμένην), loquacious, railing, given to drink, extravagant, (and tell me) if she is not worse-looking than any ugly woman?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if she were bashful, if she would hold her peace, if she learnt to blush, if to speak modestly (συμμέτρως), if to find time for fastings; her beauty would be twice as great, her freshness would be heightened, her look more engaging, fraught with modesty and good breeding (σωφροσύνης καὶ κοσμιότητος).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now then, shall we speak of men?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What can be uglier than a man in drink?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He is an object of ridicule to his servants, of ridicule to his enemies, of pity to his friends; deserving condemnation without end: a wild beast rather than a human being; for to devour much food is proper to panther, and lion, and bear.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No wonder (that they do so), for those creatures have not a reasonable soul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet even they, if they be gorged with food more than they need, and beyond the measure appointed them by nature, get their whole body ruined by it: how much more we?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore hath God contracted our stomach into a small compass; therefore hath He marked out a small measure of sustenance, that He may instruct us to attend to the soul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us consider our very make, and we shall see there is in us but one little part that has this operation—for our mouth and tongue are meant for singing hymns, our throat for voice—therefore the very necessity of nature has tied us down, that we may not, even involuntarily, get into much trouble (πραγματείαν) (in this way).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since, if indeed luxurious living had not its pains, nor sickness and infirmities, it might be tolerated: but as the case is, He hath stinted thee by restrictions of nature, that even if thou wish to exceed, thou mayest not be able to do so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is not pleasure thine object, beloved?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This thou shalt find from moderation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is not health?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This too thou shalt so gain.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is not easiness of mind?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is not freedom? is not vigor and good habit of body, is not sobriety and alertness of mind?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (All these thou shalt find); so entirely are all good things there, while in the other are the contraries to these, discomfort, distemper, disease, embarrassment—waste of substance (ἀνελευθερία).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then how comes it, you will ask, that we all run eagerly after this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It comes of disease.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For say, what is it that makes the sick man hanker after the thing that does him harm?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is not this very hankering a part of his disease?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why is it that the lame man does not walk upright?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This very thing, does it come of his being lazy, and not choosing to go to the physician?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For there are some things, in which the pleasure they bring with them is temporary, but lasting the punishment: others just the contrary, in which the endurance is for a time, the pleasure perpetual.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He, therefore, that has so little solidity and strength of purpose as not to slight present sweets for future, is soon overcome.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say, how came Esau to be overcome? how came he to prefer the present pleasure to the future honor?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Through want of solidity and firmness of character.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And this fault itself, say you, whence comes it?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of our ownselves: and it is plain from this consideration.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When we have the mind, we do rouse ourselves, and become capable of endurance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Certain it is, if at any time necessity comes upon us, nay, often only from a spirit of emulation, we get to see clearly what is useful for us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When therefore thou art about to indulge in luxury, consider how brief the pleasure, consider the loss—for loss it is indeed to spend so much money to one’s own hurt—the diseases, the infirmities: and despise luxury.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How many shall I enumerate who have suffered evils from indulgence?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Noah was drunken, and was exposed in his nakedness, and see what evils came of this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Esau through greediness abandoned his birthright, and was set upon fratricide.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The people of Israel “sat down to eat and to drink, and rose up to play.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore saith the Scripture, “When thou hast eaten and drunken, remember the Lord thy God.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For they fell over a precipice, in failing into luxury.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The widow,” he saith, “that liveth in pleasure, is dead while she liveth” (1 Tim. v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 6): and again, “The beloved waxed sleek, grew thick, and kicked” (Deut. xxxii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 15): and again the Apostle, “Make not provision for the flesh, to fulfil the lusts thereof.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I am not enacting as a law that there shall be fasting, for indeed there is no one who would listen; but I am doing away with daintiness, I am cutting off luxury for the sake of your own profit: for like a winter torrent, luxury overthrows all: there is nothing to stop its course: it casts out from a kingdom: what is the gain of it (τί τὸ πλέον)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Would you enjoy a (real) luxury?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Give to the poor; invite Christ, so that even after the table is removed, you may still have this luxury to enjoy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For now, indeed, you have it not, and no wonder: but then you will have it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Would you taste a (real) luxury?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nourish your soul, give to her of that food to which she is used: do not kill her by starvation.—It is the time for war, the time for contest: and do you sit enjoying yourself?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you not see even those who wield sceptres, how they live frugally while abroad on their campaigns?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “We wrestle not against flesh and blood” (Eph. vi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 12); and are you fattening yourself when about to wrestle?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The adversary stands grinding his teeth, and are you giving a loose to jollity, and devoting yourself to the table?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I know that I speak these things in vain, yet not (in vain) for all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He that hath ears to hear, let him hear.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Christ is pining through hunger, and are you frittering yourself away (διασπᾅς) with gluttony?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Two inconsistencies (Δύο ἀμετρίαι).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For what evil does not luxury cause?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is contrary to itself: so that I know not how it gets its name: but just as that is called glory, which is (really) infamy, and that riches, which in truth is poverty, so the name of luxury is given to that which in reality is nauseousness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do we intend ourselves for the shambles, that we so fatten ourselves?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why cater for the worm that it may have a sumptuous larder?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why make more of their humors (ἰχὥρας)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why store up in yourself sources of sweat and rank smelling?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why make yourself useless for everything?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you wish your eye to be strong?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Get your body well strung?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For in musical strings, that which is coarse and not refined, is not fit to produce musical tones, but that which has been well scraped, stretches well, and vibrates with full harmony.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why do you bury the soul alive? why make the wall about it thicker?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why increase the reek and the cloud, with fumes like a mist steaming up from all sides?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If none other, let the wrestlers teach you, that the more spare the body, the stronger it is: and (then) also the soul is more vigorous.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In fact, it is like charioteer and horse.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But there you see, just as in the case of men giving themselves to luxury, and making themselves plump, so the plump horses are unwieldy, and give the driver much ado.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: One may think one’s self (ἀγαπητὸν) well off, even with a horse obedient to the rein and well-limbed, to be able to carry off the prize: but when the driver is forced to drag the horse along, and when the horse falls, though he goad him ever so much, he cannot make him get up, be he ever so skilful himself, he will be deprived of the victory.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then let us not endure to see our soul wronged because of the body, but let us make the soul herself more clear-sighted, let us make her wing light, her bonds looser: let us feed her with discourse, with frugality, (feeding) the body only so much that it may be healthy, that it may be vigorous, that it may rejoice and not be in pain: that having in this sort well ordered our concerns, we may be enabled to lay hold upon the highest virtue, and to attain unto the eternal good things by the grace and loving-kindness of our Lord Jesus Christ, with Whom, to the Father and Holy Ghost together, be glory, dominion, honor, now and ever, world without end.
